---
title: "JSON & Web APIs: Acquiring Semi-Structured Data"
unit: "II"
lesson: "04"
type: concept
tags: [json, web-api, requests, semi-structured, normalization]
difficulty: introductory
duration: "45 mins"
---

## Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you will be able to:

- **Describe** JSON's types and how nesting encodes structure that a flat table cannot. *(Understand)*
- **Acquire** data from a web API with `requests`: parameters, response, status codes. *(Apply)*
- **Transform** nested JSON into a flat, analysis-ready table and query it. *(Apply)*

> **Where this sits:** after **L03 — CSV vs Parquet** · before **L05 — NoSQL: When Tables Don't Fit**.

> **Hands-on:** [Lab — Air Quality from a Live API](l04_lab_air_quality_api.md) ·
> [![Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/assets/colab-badge.svg)](https://colab.research.google.com/github/{{< var repo.org >}}/{{< var repo.name >}}/blob/{{< var repo.branch >}}/u02_big_data/l04_lab_air_quality_api.ipynb)

## Why This Matters

L01 asked: *which Humacao-area municipality had the worst air quality — and how sure can
we be?* — and promised that stage 2, **Acquire**, would become real in Unit II. Today it
does. The data you need usually isn't a file someone hands you; it sits behind a **web
API**, and it arrives as **JSON** — the semi-structured shape from L01's data-sources
table. In the lab you will pull real PM2.5 readings for three municipalities and produce
L01's ranked answer, coverage caveat included.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
A web API is a restaurant counter. You don't walk into the kitchen (the provider's
database) — you order from the menu (the documented endpoints and parameters), and the
kitchen decides the plating (the JSON layout). The menu is a *contract*: same order, same
dish.
:::

## JSON: Structure Without a Schema

JSON (JavaScript Object Notation) is plain text built from just six types — and that is
the whole language:

| JSON type | Looks like | Becomes in Python |
|-----------|------------|-------------------|
| object | `{"name": "Humacao"}` | `dict` |
| array | `[3.7, 3.8, 3.9]` | `list` |
| string | `"pm2_5"` | `str` |
| number | `18.1496` | `int` / `float` |
| boolean | `true` / `false` | `True` / `False` |
| null | `null` | `None` |

The power is **nesting**: objects inside arrays inside objects, to any depth. A flat
table can't hold "a municipality *containing* a list of 336 hourly readings" without
repeating the municipality on every row — JSON just nests it:

```python
# Illustrative only (read-only) — the lab works with the real thing.
import json

text = """
{
  "municipality": "Humacao",
  "coords": {"lat": 18.1496, "lon": -65.8276},
  "readings": [3.7, 3.8, 3.9]
}
"""
doc = json.loads(text)          # parse: text in, Python objects out
doc["coords"]["lat"]            # navigate the nesting with keys -> 18.1496
```

This is exactly what **semi-structured** meant in L01: organized (keys, nesting,
parseable) but not a fixed rows-and-columns schema. Each document can have its own shape
— a flexibility CSV cannot offer, and (recall L03) another case of a format trading
universality against structure.

## Web APIs: Asking a Server for Data

An API (Application Programming Interface) request is a URL with **query parameters**;
the response is a **status code** plus a JSON body:

```{mermaid}
sequenceDiagram
    participant C as Your notebook (client)
    participant S as API server
    C->>S: GET /v1/air-quality?latitude=18.15&hourly=pm2_5
    S-->>C: 200 OK + JSON body
```

Reading the diagram: one round trip. The client states *what* it wants entirely through
the URL's parameters; the server answers with a machine-readable verdict (the status
code) before you ever look at the data.

The three parts that matter:

| Part | What it is | Example |
|------|-----------|---------|
| endpoint | the base URL — *which service* | `https://air-quality-api.open-meteo.com/v1/air-quality` |
| query parameters | key=value pairs — *what exactly* | `latitude=18.15`, `hourly=pm2_5` |
| status code | the server's verdict | `200` OK · `404` not found · `429` slow down · `500` server error |

In Python, the `requests` library handles the encoding and the round trip:

```python
# Illustrative only (read-only) — the lab runs this for real.
import requests

r = requests.get(endpoint, params={"latitude": 18.1496, "longitude": -65.8276,
                                   "hourly": "pm2_5"})
r.status_code      # 200 means "here is your data"
data = r.json()    # parse the body straight into dicts and lists
```

::: {.callout-important}
**Always check the status code before trusting the body.** A `404` or `429` response
often still has a body — an error message — and code that skips the check happily
"analyzes" it. `r.raise_for_status()` turns any non-2xx answer into a visible error.
:::

## From JSON to a Table

Analysis tools want flat tables; APIs send nested trees. The bridge is **flattening**
(also called *normalizing*): walk the nesting and lay it out as columns, repeating the
parent values down the rows.

```python
# The API's nested answer...
{"municipality": "Humacao", "hourly": {"time": ["00:00", "01:00"], "pm2_5": [3.7, 3.8]}}

# ...flattened to rows a GROUP BY can chew on:
#   municipality   time    pm25
#   Humacao        00:00   3.7
#   Humacao        01:00   3.8
```

Two parallel arrays (`time` and `pm2_5`) become two columns; the municipality is repeated
down its rows. That repetition is the price of flatness — and the reason the nested form
existed in the first place. Once flat, everything from L02 applies unchanged: the lab
finishes with the same `GROUP BY` you already know, now answering L01's question on data
you acquired yourself.

## When an API Beats a File Download

- **Use the API when:** the data updates continuously, you need a precise slice (three
  towns, two weeks, one variable), or the dataset is far larger than your question.
- **Prefer a file when:** a curated snapshot exists (CSV/Parquet from L03), you need
  bit-for-bit reproducibility frozen in time, or you'd hammer the API for data that
  never changes.
- The lab splits the difference: it fetches live, then **saves the response to disk** —
  acquire once, analyze many times.

## Quiz Hooks

*Feeds the retrieval quiz at the start of the next session.*

- Why nesting makes JSON semi-structured rather than tabular <!-- obj 1 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- The parts of a GET request and what common status codes mean <!-- obj 2 · Understand · Moodle MC -->
- What flattening does to nested JSON (and what gets repeated) <!-- obj 3 · Apply · Moodle MC -->

## FAQ / Industry Reality

**"Can I just hit the API in a loop, as fast as possible?"** — No. Public APIs enforce
**rate limits** (that's status `429`); polite clients batch requests, cache responses to
disk, and identify themselves. Professionals treat an API's terms like a license: the
free tier that powers your project survives because users don't abuse it.

**"Is this the same as web scraping?"** — No. An API is a *published contract* for
machines: stable parameters, structured JSON, documented limits. Scraping parses pages
meant for humans — it breaks when the page layout changes and may violate a site's terms.
Always look for an API first; scrape only when there is none, and check the rules.

## Where to Go Deeper

- **Official docs:** [requests — Quickstart](https://requests.readthedocs.io/en/latest/user/quickstart/) — params, status codes, `.json()`, all of today's surface.
- **Explainer:** [MDN — An overview of HTTP](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Overview) — the request/response cycle one level deeper.
- **The lab's data source:** [Open-Meteo Air Quality API docs](https://open-meteo.com/en/docs/air-quality-api) — a model API reference; see how the lab's parameters are documented.
